channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Can a Federal Plan Fix Dismal Penn Station? There’s Hope.
item.description: Everyone agrees that Penn Station is a disaster, but not on how to fix it. Now a Trump-backed, multibillion-dollar plan to rebuild it offers a promising step forward.
item.dc:creator: Michael Kimmelman and Tom Wilson

item.title: Mariska Hargitay on ‘Every Brilliant Thing’ and Finding Inspiration From Jalen Brunson
item.description: The “Law & Order: SVU” actress “was really scared” about starring in “Every Brilliant Thing.” Who does she look to for inspiration? The Knicks, of course.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:credit: Lila Barth for The New York Times

item.title: ‘Toy Story 5’ Review: The Machines Have Come for the Children
item.description: In this latest installment of the popular franchise, Woody and Buzz Lightyear are back and must contend with Bonnie’s mesmerizing new toy.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: Giacometti’s Goddesses Have Entered the Temple
item.description: You may stand a little straighter after visiting the Met’s Temple of Dendur, which has been reanimated with sculptures by the Swiss master of figurative tension.
item.dc:creator: Karen Rosenberg and Daniel Terna
item.media:credit: Succession Alberto Giacometti/Artists Rights Society (ARS), NY
item.media:description: “Giacometti in the Temple of Dendur” includes more than a dozen sculptures making a rare journey abroad. Here, the 1932 bronze goddess-figure “Walking Woman (I),” a headless, stylized female that preceded his postwar attenuated, existential figures.

item.title: Netflix Cancels Duffer Brothers’ ‘The Boroughs’ After One Season
item.description: The Duffer brothers, who produced the series and created “Stranger Things,” recently left the streamer for Paramount.
item.dc:creator: Reggie Ugwu

item.title: This Season of ‘Love Island U.S.A.’ Is Giving Fans the Ick
item.description: Contestants on the hit reality dating show are participating in scenarios racy enough to turn some viewers off.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:credit: Ben Symons/Peacock
item.media:description: In one episode, “Love Island U.S.A.” contestants were handcuffed and instructed to identify their partners by touch only.

item.title: Yves Saint Laurent and Photography: A Hot and Heavy Romance
item.description: When fashion met image, both changed. A show at International Center of Photography shines a light on the relationship — but doesn’t really get the whole picture.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman

item.title: At Art Basel, a Nervy, Make-or-Break Mood
item.description: The auction market is buoyant, but business feels precarious for dealers at the world’s premier contemporary art fair.
item.dc:creator: Scott Reyburn

item.title: ‘The Death of Robin Hood’ Review: Not a Merry Band
item.description: A darkly revisionist take on the medieval outlaw asks who gets to craft the heroic legends.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: Matthew Rhys, of ‘Widow’s Bay,’ Is on a Boat. Barely.
item.description: The star of this seafaring horror-comedy, which wraps up Season 1 on Wednesday, also spends his downtime on the water.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski and Blaise Cepis
item.media:credit: Blaise Cepis for The New York Times

item.title: Bruce Nauman on How to Make Art: Don’t Try
item.description: The artist’s latest videos and drawings stem from physical and mental exercises and are largely improvised in his studio, with raw and vulnerable results.
item.dc:creator: Travis Diehl

item.title: As the Job Market Stutters, Simulated Work Is Surging
item.description: By asking players to perform repetitive labor in mundane settings, game designers are delivering relatable twists on the tycoon genre.
item.dc:creator: Lewis Gordon

item.title: Cory Stearns, American Ballet Theater Star, on Retirement and the Pains of Aging
item.description: Stearns, a longtime principal at American Ballet Theater, has stepped down. He talks about being pushed to leave and the pains and boons of growing older.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney and Paola Chapdelaine
item.media:description: “I am not as buoyant as I used to be,” Stearns said. At the same time, he added, “My understanding of human emotion and how to convey those emotions is much better than it used to be.”

item.title: Hagen Quartet Is Taking Its Final Bows After 45 Years
item.description: Formed by siblings who grew up playing together, the Hagen Quartet is finishing a farewell tour with an unforced, casual goodbye.
item.dc:creator: Jeffrey Arlo Brown

item.title: To Take Us to the Heavens, James Turrell Went Underground
item.description: The American artist’s most ambitious “skyspace” installation, opening in Denmark on Friday, invites viewers to connect with the cosmos both inner and outer.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Abend
item.media:credit: Mathias Eis for The New York Times
item.media:description: In the installation, said the architect Morten Schmidt, “you look directly up into the cosmos. But at the same time, it makes you reflect inward.”

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Calls Trump’s Iran Deal ‘The Retreaty of Versailles’
item.description: “Trump’s answer to the question, ‘Who did you go to war with?’ and ‘How did it end?’ will be the same answer: ‘Iran,’” Kimmel said.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: ‘Voicemails for Isabelle’ Review: An Unexpected Recipient
item.description: The movie, starring Zoey Deutch and Nick Robinson, begins as a tear-jerker and morphs into a poignant rom-com.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Kenny

item.title: While Knicks Fans Celebrate, a New York Artist Fumes
item.description: After the Knicks promoted Gavin Snider’s watercolor painting in the wake of their N.B.A. title, one of the most famous artists on social media posted an uncannily similar work.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small and Frank Reneau

item.title: Man Who Tried to Enter Sabrina Carpenter’s Home Gets 5-Year Restraining Order
item.description: The pop star and songwriter said she feared what the man would do if he was not prevented from coming near her or her family, according to court records.
item.dc:creator: Aimee Ortiz
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sabrina Carpenter said the man involved was “a complete stranger to me,” and said she had “no desire to meet him or communicate with him.”

item.title: Danny Simmons, Painter and Activist From a Creative Family, Dies at 72
item.description: The older brother of the music mogul Russell Simmons and the rapper Joseph Simmons, he made his own way as an artistic and entrepreneurial force in Brooklyn.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: Ruby Washington/The New York Times
item.media:description: Danny Simmons at his home in Brooklyn in 2004. “Danny’s the fine artist” in the family, his brother Russell Simmons told The New York Times in 2004. “The real thing, a cultural hero.”

item.title: Outdoor Sculpture Shows Feature Henry Moore, Lynn Chadwick in England This Summer
item.description: As art lovers head to London for the Treasure House Fair, outdoor sculpture shows featuring Henry Moore, Lynn Chadwick and big names beckon.
item.dc:creator: Scott Reyburn
item.category: Goodwood Art Foundation (West Sussex, England, Museum)

item.title: The Treasure House Fair Arrives at the Right Time in London
item.description: The Treasure House Fair returns for its fourth year, embracing accessibility and emotion over connoisseurship.
item.dc:creator: Dale Berning Sawa
item.media:credit: Jeremie Souteyrat for The New York Times
item.media:description: London, with its well-known locations like the Palace of Westminster’s Elizabeth Tower, which houses Big Ben, has traditionally drawn art dealers and collectors in late June.

item.title: The Artist Cao Fei Weighs How We Can Preserve Our Humanity in the Age of A.I.
item.description: In a major show at the Kunstmuseum Basel, Gegenwart, the artist considers technology, humanity and how people can preserve “our human life” in the age of A.I.
item.dc:creator: Farah Nayeri

item.title: The Mexican Artist Ramòn Saturnino Is Breaking Down Walls
item.description: Ramón Saturnino, an architect by training, is showing his work on the U.S.-Mexico border wall for the first time at Art Basel.
item.dc:creator: Ray Mark Rinaldi
item.media:credit: Fred Ramos for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ramón Saturnino, a Mexican architect and artist, is looking to create art that tells a story beyond his politics.

item.title: ‘Unidentified’ Review: A High-Concept Murder Mystery
item.description: The director Haifaa Al-Mansour (“Wadjda”) concludes a trilogy about women resisting societal constraints in Saudi Arabia with a whodunit.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg

item.title: ‘Rose of Nevada’ Review: Out of the Mists of Time and Back Again
item.description: In this richly textured film by Mark Jenkin, two men sign on to crew a ship whose ultimate destination is a place they could never have fathomed.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Yu

item.title: ‘Maddie’s Secret’ Review: She’s Got the Sauce
item.description: A food influencer struggles with an eating disorder in John Early’s thrilling pastiche of the made-for-TV melodrama.
item.dc:creator: Natalia Winkelman

item.title: ‘Girls Like Girls’ Review: Hayley Kiyoko Brings Her Song to Life
item.description: The pop star Hayley Kiyoko turns her hit single and best-selling book into a coming-of-age romance.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Kennedy

item.title: ‘Leviticus’ Review: The Shocking Shapes That Love Can Take
item.description: In this Australian chiller, a conversion therapy ritual unleashes shape-shifting ghouls that take the form of desired ones.
item.dc:creator: Beatrice Loayza

item.title: ‘Color Book’ Review: Love and Grieving
item.description: In this tender family drama, a widowed father and his disabled son overcome personal tragedy.
item.dc:creator: Robert Daniels

item.title: ‘Never Change!’ Review: Failing the Assignment
item.description: This comedy squanders a promising premise about adults forced back to high school on repetitive sketches and hollow jokes.
item.dc:creator: Chris Azzopardi

item.title: ‘The Devil Queen’ Review: A Seedy Quest for Vengeance
item.description: Antonio Carlos da Fontoura’s 1974 gay gangster thriller returns in a gorgeous new restoration.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg

item.title: Critics of Russia Say This Critic Isn’t Critical Enough
item.description: Alexander Sokurov has questioned the Russian president about government repression, but he is still controversial among his country’s exiles.
item.dc:creator: Milana Mazaeva and Neil MacFarquhar
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: Alexander Sokurov during the screening of his latest film, “Director’s Diary,” in Paris, in March.

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Feels ‘Hormuzled’ by Trump’s New Deal With Iran
item.description: Although the agreement finally reopens the Strait of Hormuz, “we threw in a minimum of $300 billion,” Kimmel said. “Right now, Melania’s wondering, ‘How do I get a deal like that?’”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Daveigh Chase, ‘Lilo & Stitch’ Voice Actor and ‘The Ring’ Villain, Dies at 35
item.description: She brought the plucky Lilo to life in the popular animated film series, and unnerved viewers as the child villain Samara in the horror film “The Ring.”
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald
item.media:credit: Jason Merritt/Getty Images
item.media:description: Daveigh Chase in 2011. Her father said she had struggled with drugs since the age of 13.

item.title: Robert Thurman, Leading Expert on Tibetan Buddhism and Uma Thurman’s Father, Dies at 84
item.description: A former monk who was also Uma Thurman’s father, he made sure Buddhism retained its intellectual and spiritual rigor as it spread through the West.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: Astrid Stawiarz/Getty Images for Tibet House
item.media:description: Robert Thurman in 2015. He brought an infectious energy to the endless lectures and conferences he organized around Buddhism.

item.title: ‘Matlock’ Writer Sues CBS, Claiming Hostile Environment for Black Employees
item.description: Some of the show’s leadership made harassing statements about the writer’s race and sexuality, the lawsuit says. CBS said it had been unable to find support for the allegations.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor

item.title: Mystikal, Louisiana Rapper, Sentenced to 20 Years in Rape Case
item.description: The Grammy-nominated musician pleaded guilty to third-degree rape after a woman told the police she was beaten and assaulted at his home in 2022.
item.dc:creator: Reggie Ugwu
item.media:credit: Rusty Costanza/Associated Press
item.media:description: The rapper Mystikal in Baton Rouge, La., in 2021.

item.title: The Good List: 6 Things to Bring Joy to Your Day
item.description: A memory workout, an endless playlist and an antidote to worry.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Carlo Ginzburg, Who Told the History of the Obscure, Dies at 87
item.description: In books like “The Cheese and the Worms,” he helped push beyond the story of great events and leaders, entering the minds and hearts of peasants.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Kandell
item.media:credit: Leonardo Cendamo/Getty Images
item.media:description: Carlo Ginzburg in Bologna, Italy, in 1996. When he embarked on his research in the 1960s, there was little interest in discovering what the everyday people of centuries past thought and believed.

item.title: ‘Curse of the Seven Jackals’: A Film Made to Be Exhumed
item.description: Anthology Film Archives is screening Chris Jolly’s restored 2001 lo-fi indie film twice nightly.
item.dc:creator: J. Hoberman
item.media:credit: Anthology Film Archives
item.media:description: Bernard (Kevin Barnes from the band Of Montreal) signs on to test an experimental form of synthetic blood.

item.title: Late Night Wades Into the Reflecting Pool’s Algae Bloom
item.description: President Trump “promised he would drain the swamp,” Jimmy Kimmel said. “Instead, he spent 14 million of our dollars building a new one.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Judge Orders Kennedy Center to Make a Plan for Staying Open
item.description: As the Trump administration seeks to move forward with renovations at the center, a judge has asked for its programming calendar.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Alex Kent/The New York Times
item.media:description: The John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts has three days to update a federal judge on whether it still intends to push for a two-year closure for renovations, a decision he has temporarily blocked.

item.title: Gregory Williams, Academic With an Uncommon Perspective on Race, Dies at 81
item.description: As a child, he discovered that his father — and therefore he and his siblings — had been passing for white. For the rest of his life, he identified as Black.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:credit: Matt Moyer for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gregory Williams in 2002 at the City College of New York, during his tenure as its president. He also served as dean of the law school at the Ohio State University and president of the University of Cincinnati.

item.title: ‘Are You Now or Have You Ever Been’ Review: Who Is Naming Names?
item.description: Anna D. Shapiro revives Eric Bentley’s play about the House Un-American Activities Committee’s investigation of the entertainment industry.
item.dc:creator: Laura Collins-Hughes
item.category: Are You Now or Have You Ever Been (Play)

item.title: 7 Great Artists Playing SummerStage This Year
item.description: Hear songs from Angélique Kidjo, Mavis Staples, Sharp Pins and more.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:credit: Nina Westervelt for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mavis Staples, left, returns to SummerStage on July 16.

item.title: Tell Us Why Taylor Swift’s Wedding Is Meaningful to You
item.description: For an upcoming story, we want fans to talk to us about the journey they have been on with Swift through the years and how they feel about her big day.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:credit: David Eulitt/Getty Images
item.media:description: Taylor Swift and Travis Kelce will be tying the knot in the near future.

item.title: In 30,000 Gallons of Water, Hundreds of Bowls Play Music of the Spheres
item.description: The French sound artist Céleste Boursier-Mougenot creates an unlikely symphony by setting singing bowls bobbing at the Park Avenue Armory.
item.dc:creator: Walker Mimms and Guarionex Rodriguez
item.media:credit: Guarionex Rodriguez for The New York Times

item.title: The ‘Paddington’ Musical Will Come to Broadway Next Spring
item.description: The show, which revisits the story of a marmalade-loving bear, plans to open next April at the Hirschfeld Theater in New York.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Kalpesh Lathigra for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Paddington,” according to the musical’s producer Sonia Friedman, is “a show about fun, adventure, joy and comedy, but on a more profound level it’s a story about searching for home.”

item.title: Sharleen Chidiac Brings Punk and Excess in New Musical ‘All the Rage’
item.description: Sharleen Chidiac, a choreographer and the effervescent lead singer of Voyeur, looks at ambition and freedom in her hybrid new work: a punk musical.
item.dc:creator: Gia Kourlas and George Etheredge
item.media:credit: George Etheredge for The New York Times

item.title: A Baroque Opera Written By a Female Composer Feels Ripped From the Headlines
item.description: The Paris Opera production of “Ercole Amante” (“Hercules in Love”), a rediscovered 1707 work by the female composer Antonia Bembo, leans into contemporary themes of coercion and consent.
item.dc:creator: Roslyn Sulcas

item.title: Best Classical Music and Opera of 2026 So Far
item.description: Kaija Saariaho’s final opera, a premiere by Ellen Reid and a multitasking performance by Barbara Hannigan are among the highlights.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone

item.title: How Artisans Are Recreating the American Revolution
item.description: In the spirit of 1776, these shipwrights, printers and textile workers are commemorating George Washington’s legacy through 18th-century techniques.
item.dc:creator: Lise Funderburg and Hannah Yoon

item.title: Knicks Celebrate Their N.B.A. Title on ‘The Tonight Show’
item.description: “Of course it’s the Knicks, so they won’t really show up until the second half,” the host Jimmy Fallon said.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Dawn Richard’s Lawsuit Against Sean Combs Is Dismissed
item.description: Ms. Richard, a singer who once worked with the music mogul, accused him of creating an abusive work environment. The judge said her claims came too late.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:credit: Jordan Strauss/Invision, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Sean Combs is still facing dozens of lawsuits that accused him of sexual misconduct, but a federal judge dismissed one, largely on statute of limitations grounds.

item.title: In Its Tragic Finale, ‘Romeo and Juliet’ Is Interrupted by a Cat
item.description: Performers were tested by the unexpected during a ballet production in Turkey.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather
item.media:credit: Tanya Borger, via Reuters

item.title: America250 Plans July 4 Concert in L.A., Competing With Trump
item.description: The nonprofit, established by Congress a decade ago and separate from President Trump’s Freedom 250, will bring Smashing Pumpkins and other acts to Los Angeles on July 4.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Frazer Harrison/Getty Images
item.media:description: Billy Corgan and his band Smashing Pumpkins will celebrate America’s 250th anniversary with a performance on July 4 in Los Angeles.

item.title: Abdullah Ibrahim, Eminent South African Jazz Pianist, Dies at 91
item.description: His song “Mannenberg” became the unofficial anthem of the country’s fight against apartheid, with Nelson Mandela calling him “our Mozart.”
item.dc:creator: Giovanni Russonello
item.media:credit: Christian Rose/Roger Viollet, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Mr. Ibrahim in 1995. Many U.S. record companies shied away from him in part because of his outspoken politics. Instead, he recorded most often for Enja, a German label.

item.title: Ronald LaPread, Funky Bassist for the Commodores, Dies at 76
item.description: A talented musician, he lied when asked by Lionel Richie, the lead singer, if he played bass. Then he taught himself how, and was essential to the band’s success.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:credit: Mike Prior/Redferns, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Ronald LaPread, right, performing with the Commodores in 1978. He joined the band in 1970 and remained a member for 16 years, contributing to multiple Top 10 singles and albums.

item.title: What Is Contemporary Dance? Lincoln Center Invests in Finding Out
item.description: Thanks to a $50 million grant, a new festival has been born. Kyle Abraham helps to curate the first edition, which is part of the center’s Summer of Dance.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert
item.category: Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts

item.title: ‘Break Your Hips!’ Spinning to the Rhythm of Venezuelan Tambores
item.description: Tambores may not be as widely known as other Latin dances like merengue and salsa, but that is starting to change with the exodus of millions of Venezuelans.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Starr and Lucía Vázquez
item.category: Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts

item.title: From the Kennedy Center to U.F.C Fights in Trump’s Washington
item.description: A day after the National Symphony Orchestra played perhaps its final notes at the Kennedy Center for years, mixed martial artists brawled outside the White House.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs and Emmanuel Morgan
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Thousands of screaming fans watched seven Ultimate Fighting Championship bouts on the South Lawn of the White House on Sunday.
